reasoning_messages.reasoning:
We need to create plan. The question: "You are the Remedy agent remediating ONE OpenSCAP finding... This is finding: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Different Characters. Not allowed to chain commands. Use only run_cmd, read_file, write_file. We must propose plan, review_plan system expects function to evaluate. We should produce a plan first, then call review_plan with plan description. Not allowed to execute commands until approved.

We need to produce plan: modify /etc/security/pwquality.conf and possibly *.conf in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d. We'll need to backup files. Use sed to modify or append.

Goal: set difok to desired value. The rule says "Minimum Different Characters" but we don't know the mandated value. The recommended script sets var_password_pam_difok='' apparently blank? Wait, the script has var_password_pam_difok='' maybe to set to zero? But typical hardening uses difok=3 perhaps. But the rule description: "Modify the difok setting in /etc/security/pwquality.conf to require differing characters when changing passwords." Usually difok=3 maybe. We can set to 3 or maybe 3 recommended by STIG. Let's choose 3.

Thus my plan: - backup /etc/security/pwquality.conf to /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak or .orig file. - check existing difok lines in pwquality.conf. If none, add "difok = 3" at the end. If exists, comment out or replace. Use sed to replace line that starts with difok (ignore comment) replacing the value. Ensure no duplicate lines. Also, search /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf for difok entries. For each file, create backup, then use sed to delete any lines starting with difok, then append "difok = 3". Use run_cmd for each file separately. But only one command at a time. We will perform read_file to check for existing entries before modifying. However we might not need to read_file in plan? We'll include to read the file, then modify.

Also we need to output plan to review_plan call.

Thus step 1: In analysis produce plan. Then we need to call review_plan with plan_description containing full plan. Then should wait for approval. Then if approved we apply.

We need to be careful: Only one command at a time. Changing multiple files would require multiple steps, but we only need to produce plan. But we don't need to execute now. Once approved, we need to run commands. The plan may involve multiple commands. Also, as per requirement, we can't chain. So we'll prepare to run commands one by one.

We also need to backup files with write_file maybe? Use run_cmd to backup? For backup, we could run cp -p or maybe use read_file then write_file. But maybe simpler: use run_cmd "cp -p /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak". But this is not allowed? It says we can use run_cmd. That's okay. But must use only one command. We'll do cp for backup.

Similarly for any *.conf: cp -p ....

We need to use read_file before modifying? Yes.

We need to avoid duplicate lines and modify existing values. Use sed.

But we can't chain on one file? For each file, we need two steps: read_file to inspect, and if needed run_cmd sed -i ... next. We can't chain sed -i/delete/append both in one command? We can do sed -i "s/^#\?\s*difok\s*=.*$//" file? Actually we want to delete the line. We can chain deletion and then append an echo? But we should not chain. So we might need to delete via sed, then use echo >> file to append new line. This would require separate commands.

Is this acceptable? Yes, we can do two commands: sed -i '/^#\?\s*difok\s*=.*$/d' file; and echo "difok = 3" | tee -a file. That's two commands. Satisfies one command at a time. That's fine.

But we must not use '&&' or ';' chaining. So separate calls.

Hence plan: 1) backup /etc/security/pwquality.conf. 2) Check if difok exists in the file. 3) If yes: comment out and set diff? Actually we just replace. Use sed to replace line. If not, we append line.

Same for conf.d/ files.

Also need to validate syntax after changes? They request verifying with pwquality --config. We can run run_cmd "pwquality -d" perhaps? Wait actual command: "pwquality --config /etc/security/pwquality.conf" maybe. The command could give error if syntax wrong. So run run_cmd "pwquality -d". The man page: "pwquality -d" is for dry run? I'm not 100% sure.

But we might skip that step as it's optional.

Do we need to show a plan with steps? Yes.

Thus in plan_description: we provide details.

Let's craft plan description.

We need to propose backup file names.

Also mention that we check for multiple conf.d files and modify each.

We'll produce planDescription accordingly.

Then call review_plan.

Let's produce plan.
